_note: Recap library for delivered ZABAL Gamez sessions. Newest first. type is one of workshop|fireside|bczworkshop (defaults to workshop if absent) and drives both the /recordings hub grouping and the /recordings/index.json AI index. recording/youtube/transcript/thumbnail optional - only include links + files that exist. page (a dedicated /recordings/... landing page) takes priority for the primary 'Watch the recording' button; otherwise youtube renders a 'Watch on YouTube' button. share_topics[] feed the randomized 'I'm watching' Farcaster/X share buttons (one topic picked at random per click). Fields: date, type, title, presenter, handle, org, track, format, thumbnail, summary, topics[], recording, youtube, page, transcript, link, link_label, takeaways[], share_topics[], chapters[] (array of {t, label} where t is seconds and the first entry is 0 - drives the clickable chapter jumps on a generated video page), okd (YYYY-MM-DD the guest reviewed the cut/page and signed off, before public release - absent until they approve), cast_hash (the root Farcaster cast for this recording - the announce cast; its replies render as the live 'Thoughts' conversation via /api/cast-comments. A 0x hash or a Warpcast URL). To publish a new recording from a ZAOVideoEditor manifest (writes transcript + recap + recordings/N.html + rebuilds the index in one step) use: node scripts/ingest-recording.mjs <manifest.json> --write. After hand-editing this file, regenerate the AI index + hub JSON-LD: node scripts/build-recordings-index.mjs.

recaps.title: ZABAL GAMEZ Workshop w/Teresa Marrin Nakra from Stevens University
recaps.format: Talk + live demo + Q and A, recorded
recaps.summary: Teresa Marrin Nakra - professor at Stevens University and inventor of the Conductor's Jacket - on making music with generative AI in Ableton using Magenta, plus a live demo. An artist-track look at where AI tools meet real music-making from someone who has been building at that intersection for decades.
takeaways: How a music-technology pioneer uses generative AI inside Ableton with Magenta.
A live demo of making music with AI tools, not just talking about them.
Perspective from decades at the intersection of music and technology (MIT Media Lab, the Conductor's Jacket).
Where AI fits into a real creative workflow for artists.

recaps.title: ZABAL GAMEZ Workshop w/topocount from Neynar
recaps.summary: topocount from Neynar goes under the hood of Snapchain, the storage and signing layer behind Farcaster - how Snapchain signers work, OG signers vs Snapchain-native signers, how storage is structured, the move to bring more of the Farcaster client (channels, blocks) onto the protocol, and where Snapchain is headed. Built for developers who want the behind-the-hood view of Farcaster infrastructure.
takeaways: Snapchain is the open social backend of Farcaster - it accepts every cast, like, and follow and distributes them to all clients, and anyone can inspect or build on it.
How Snapchain signers work, and the difference between OG signers and Snapchain-native signers.
The plan to move more of what lives in the Farcaster client - channels, blocks - onto the protocol, so other clients can build on it.
topocount's path from smart-contract / EVM engineer to working on Snapchain itself in Rust at Neynar.

Artwork that doubles as editable code - the output is also the source.
Shipping the same tool as a webpage and a Farcaster Mini App.

takeaways: Harness - the tool Saltorious built, and why he built it.
Bankr - the team he just joined and what they do.
How both fit into the way people build onchain right now.
Going from a solo builder shipping a tool to joining a team.

recaps.title: ZABAL GAMEZ Workshop w/Meta Mu from Rose City Web3
recaps.summary: Meta Mu, founder of Rose City Web3 and an artist-first builder in Portland, joins ZABAL Gamez on how to be a community member who actually adds value - not just another lurker in the chat. He breaks down how to find communities worth your time and how to spot a dead one fast, the difference between a pull-the-ladder-up culture and a help-people-up culture, how to get tapped in through Meetup, Eventbrite, and IRL movements, and exactly how to show up at an event, introduce yourself, and make a real first connection. Drawn from his own path through NFT and DAO communities, PDX DAO, and the regen movement.
takeaways: Find communities worth your time - a dead chat with the last message a week ago is a red flag.
Look for help-people-up cultures over pull-the-ladder-up ones.
Get tapped in through Meetup, Eventbrite, and IRL movements - showing up in person matters.
Do not blindly hooray projects; add real value instead.
At an event, introduce yourself and give people something to try - that is the first connection.

A hands-on look at building software with AI when you are not a traditional engineer, what previbecoding means in practice, and the lessons that carry over for anyone starting out.
takeaways: What previbecoding means in practice - building with AI tooling before you are a fluent coder.
How someone without a software background can start creating systems and code with AI.
Lessons and habits that carry over for anyone learning to code right now.

recaps.title: ZABAL GAMEZ Workshop w/Dan Singjoy from Eden Fractal
recaps.summary: Dan Singjoy, founder of Eden Fractal, breaks down the fractal ecosystem - a way for communities to make decisions together that are fair, fast, and fun, using Respect earned through weekly contributions and peer consensus. Dan shares his path from musician to building onchain, founding Eden Fractal and the Eden Creators media studio, and the Optimism Fractal origins where he and Zaal first met. He walks through how a weekly fractal runs, the tripartite governance model, claiming Respect onchain, and how ZAO Fractal now runs itself with as few as four people. It closes on a token-gated Fractal browser idea for community spaces.
takeaways: A fractal makes group decisions fair, fast, and fun - Respect earned through weekly contributions and peer consensus.
Respect is soulbound and illiquid - tied to governance, not tradable.
ZAO Fractal now runs itself with as few as four people, no host needed.
Respect unlocks governance, funding, and role allocation - not just a score.
A token-gated Fractal browser could give a community its own gated workspace.

recaps.title: ZABAL GAMEZ Workshop w/diviflyy from Empire Builder
recaps.summary: Adrian (diviflyy), co-founder and lead engineer of Empire Builder, goes under the hood of Empire Builder V3 - the docs at empirebuilder.world, the public free-to-use endpoints, and how to pull leaderboards, rankings, boosters, and reward data into your own apps. Empire Builder is the community-layer framework for any project, token-based or tokenless. Live, Adrian builds a Zabal Empire mini app in about an hour using the Empire Builder skill plus a free Farcaster data source - surfacing rewards, rankings, and boosters under your own brand. He covers getting your empire ID, the endpoint set, and where it is heading: multiple treasuries, more chains, and agent bots that run an empire. Plus build ideas for the ZABAL Gamez hackathon.
takeaways: Empire Builder is a community-layer framework for any project, token-based or tokenless.
The public, free-to-use endpoints surface top empires, boosters, distributions, and leaderboards - no key needed.
Your empire ID is your token address, or an address-based identifier for tokenless empires.
You can build a branded rewards mini app in about an hour with the Empire Builder skill and an AI assistant.
Big build ideas: a tag-to-query empire bot, an Empire Draft game, and one-shot tokenless empire deploys.

recaps.title: ZABAL GAMEZ Workshop w/Chris Dolinsky from Vini App
recaps.summary: Chris Dolinsky (1dolinski), a 15-year software builder and founder of Vini App, shows how anyone - including the people who have never written a line of code - can build a real app from a plain-language prompt. Every Vini app ships with a wallet, hosting, a domain, a brand kit, and a token, and that token can help fund the app as it grows. Chris demos the flow live: sign in with your Farcaster wallet or just an email, describe your app, and the AI handles the questions, the design, and the build. He walks Vini by the numbers, the umbrella Vini token, and standout builds like DGEN Fishing and Farcenoid - a game now live across Farcaster, World App, and Celo. Plus where Vini is headed, and a July collab to get non-coders building in the ZABAL Gamez hackathon.
takeaways: Anyone can build a real app from a plain-language prompt - Vini handles wallet, hosting, domain, brand kit, and a token.
Land and expand: start small, ship, and learn fast instead of carrying the whole idea up front.
An app token can help fund the app itself as it grows.
Sign in is seamless - your Farcaster wallet, or just an email that creates a wallet behind the scenes.
Quality ships: Farcenoid, built on Vini, went live across Farcaster, World App, and Celo MiniPay.

recaps.title: POIDH: the open bounty protocol
recaps.summary: Kenny, founder of POIDH, breaks down the open bounty protocol he built - put a bounty on-chain, transparently, and anyone can fund it, add to it, or claim it. The throughline: POIDH is a protocol, not just an app. poidh.xyz is the most popular front end, but anyone can build their own - NARS DAO launched an alternate front end the morning of the session. Zaal walks through running real bounties: the Best ZABAL Gamez Ad bounty with 8 submissions, a submitters board that queries the contract directly with an AI-judged rubric, and rewarding every submitter through an Empire Builder leaderboard. Kenny shows composability in action - Femi shipped an NFT marketplace MVP in 90 minutes off the open-source repo - then creates a new bounty live: the ZABAL Gamez Open Pot, open through July.
takeaways: POIDH is a protocol, not an app - anyone can build a front end on the same contracts, like NARS DAO did.
Open bounties let anyone add funds on top; deadlines plus single-winner curation surface real value over money-grabbers.
Composability compounds: Femi built an NFT marketplace MVP in 90 minutes from the open repo, and Vini App added POIDH the same week.
Good bounties carry maximum context - a pre-cleared brand kit, a clear rubric, a markdown description.
You can reward every participant programmatically through an Empire Builder leaderboard, not just the winner.

recaps.title: Non-Dev to Shipped: building Baraza Protocol with AI tools
recaps.summary: A ZABAL Gamez session with Aziz (MotoMoto) of Build Africa DAO. Coming from media and production with no coding background, he built Baraza Protocol by directing AI tools - a mobile-first, no-jargon platform for the savings groups and cooperatives across Africa (chamas, SACCOs, stokvels) to run on-chain, plus Baraza TV, a decentralized broadcast layer. He traces the path from a non-technical-founders hackathon (40 dollars, 72 hours, Claude and ChatGPT) to a working build, why he put mobile and accessibility first (M-PESA, USSD, zero blockchain jargon), and his advice for non-coders: know the problem you can identify with, write the PRD that answers the real questions, then just ship and build in public.
takeaways: You do not need to be a coder. Aziz came from media and production, spent about 40 dollars on Claude and ChatGPT, and shipped a working build in a 72-hour hackathon for non-technical founders.

Build for the people who will actually use it. If his mother or someone without formal education cannot use it, it does not work - so Baraza Protocol is mobile-first, runs on M-PESA and USSD, and hides all the blockchain jargon.
Meet existing communities where they are. Chamas, SACCOs, and stokvels already run on a black book; the goal is to do business with them, not change how they do business.
Sell the experience, not the technology. Like mobile money and Coca-Cola, adoption comes from the experience and the value, not from the word crypto.

For non-coders building with AI: know the problem you can identify with, write the PRD that answers the real questions first, then just ship and build in public. Time in planning saves five times the time in execution.
share_topics: shipping a real build with no coding background
building Baraza Protocol for Africa’s savings groups
why mobile-first and no jargon wins adoption
meeting communities where they already are
the non-coder path: know the problem, write the PRD, ship

recaps.title: MIDAO + Beta Briefing: legal entities for DAOs and AI agents
recaps.summary: A ZABAL Gamez conversation with Adam "The Thriller" Miller, founder and CEO of MIDAO. Two threads: MIDAO, the Marshall Islands legal framework that lets a DAO - or now a decentralized AI agent - become a real legal entity (a DAO LLC), so the people governing it are not personally liable for what it does; and Beta Briefing, his agentic daily-news tool that runs a multi-stage AI pipeline over your interests and delivers an editorialized briefing in text and audio. Plus a practical agentic-engineering segment: directing Claude Code with Codex as a second opinion and a dual-review sub-agent command.
takeaways: A DAO LLC (Marshall Islands) gives a DAO legal personhood and limited liability - the people governing it are not personally on the hook for the entity's actions.
The same structure now works for a decentralized AI agent: governed by people, but the agent is the manager - it can sign contracts and open bank accounts, and can be elected in or out.
If you do not elect a legal structure, the government applies a default one, usually not the one you want. Tokens and DAOs need documents.
Beta Briefing runs an 11-plus stage agentic pipeline over your profile to deliver a daily, editorialized news briefing in text and audio (RSS too).

Agentic-engineering tip: have Claude Code call Codex (and Google's CLI) for second opinions, build a dual-review sub-agent command, and keep upgrading your Claude.md and skills so it runs longer autonomously.
share_topics: making your DAO a real legal entity
giving a decentralized AI agent legal personhood
why you can't skip a legal structure
Beta Briefing, an agentic daily-news pipeline
Claude Code plus Codex dual-review

recaps.title: The Farcaster protocol, ins and outs
recaps.summary: Cassie (Cassandra Heart, founder of Quilibrium and a contributor to Farcaster) gives a deep, candid tour of the Farcaster protocol: what 'sufficiently decentralized' really meant, the hubs era and its scaling limits, and the rewrite into Snapchain. She lays out the two centralization problems (the validator set, controlled mostly by Neynar; and the fact that you can't build a real client straight off Snapchain), then walks through her Hypersnap fork as an additive side chain: threshold ECDSA via MPC, the Snap token and gasless bridging, consensus (Malachite/Tendermint, one-second blocks and the speed-of-light limit), the external OP Mainnet contracts (ID, storage, key, FNAME registries), gasless signers, spam/trust scoring, and the roadmap. Closes with the five North Stars for getting Farcaster to a million daily actives - chief among them, making it possible to build a client in a weekend.
takeaways: Farcaster's data layer, Snapchain, is a real shard-based BFT blockchain; its validator set is mostly Neynar today, which is the live centralization question.
You can't build a meaningful client straight off Snapchain - you need your own indexer and backend. Making a weekend-buildable client is the top goal.
Hypersnap is an additive side chain: threshold ECDSA via MPC, the Snap token, and gasless bridging to any EVM chain, without breaking Snapchain.
External anchors matter: the OP Mainnet ID/storage/key/FNAME registries, and the new gasless signers (cost-free revoke/add) born from a real signer-compromise incident.
The five North Stars: a real query layer (done), a template+SDK for clients, channels/curation as first-class, mini app discovery at the protocol level, and native monetization.
share_topics: why you can't just build a Farcaster client off Snapchain
Snapchain vs Hypersnap, explained
building a Farcaster client in a weekend
the Snap token and gasless bridging
the five North Stars for Farcaster's next million users
gasless signers and why they exist

recaps.title: Earn from music battles, not streams: WaveWarz
recaps.summary: Hurricane Ike, founder of WaveWarz, on a new way for musicians to get paid: head-to-head music battles where people buy and sell on the competing tracks like stocks, and the artist earns one percent of every trade on their side - engagement, not streams. One artist earned about 0.5 SOL in minutes of a single battle. He covers the live chart scoring that makes a battle as fun to watch as it is to hear, the nightly 8:30pm EST quick-battle queue, and community battles in any format (music, roast, cooking) with automated payouts straight to artist wallets. Artists keep their masters, IP, and audience. 500+ SOL in volume, 40+ artists, built on Solana.
takeaways: Artists earn from engagement - one percent of every trade on their side of a battle - not from streams.
The live charts make a battle a game: the score moves as people buy and sell.
You keep your masters, IP, and audience. WaveWarz never holds them.
Quick battles run nightly at 8:30pm EST; anyone can queue one for 0.05 SOL.
Community battles run any format - music, roast, cooking - with automated, split payouts.
500+ SOL in volume, 40+ artists, built on Solana for fast, low-fee trades.

the 1% trade cut on every battle
the live charts that make a battle a game
keeping your masters, IP, and audience

what you can build on top of WaveWarz

recaps.title: Building KFMEDIA
recaps.summary: A creator-track conversation with Will T of Web3 on building KFMEDIA (Kingfishers Media) - a media company that helps independent content creators distribute their work using on-chain tools, without learning to code. When Bonfire.xyz sunset and CharmVerse announced the same, Will (a self-described non-coder) learned to vibe code and forked CharmVerse to rebuild the Kingfishers Media learning center on its own terms. The practical core: the three tools KFMEDIA actually uses to distribute creator media - Unlock Protocol for subscriptions, memberships, and booking pages (fiat or ERC-20); Highlight.xyz, which stores NFT video permanently on Arweave; and Contra for Web2 reach - plus KFM Talks, a TEDx-style independent creator program that returns 80 to 85% of sales to the creator. Kingfishers Media is moving to a true public good, dropping token gating and converting to a nonprofit association in Q4.
takeaways: You do not have to be a coder to own your stack. Will, a self-described creative founder with no ambition to code, learned to vibe code and forked CharmVerse to rebuild the Kingfishers Media learning center when the tool they relied on was sunsetting.

Relying on a third-party service is a risk: Bonfire.xyz sunset with no open-source fallback, and CharmVerse announced the same. Open-sourcing a tool as a public good (like Hats Protocol did) lets the people who depended on it keep going.

Unlock Protocol does far more than event RSVPs - KFMEDIA uses it for creator subscriptions (monthly to annual), memberships, and even booking pages, accepting fiat via Stripe or ERC-20 tokens like USDC.

For permanent media, KFMEDIA distributes NFT video through Highlight.xyz, which writes the files to Arweave so the content outlives the platform. Contra covers Web2 reach. Pick tools that match the media and the audience.

KFM Talks is a TEDx-style program built entirely on Web3 tooling that returns 80 to 85% of sales to the creator (versus 30 to 40% in a traditional agency), and Kingfishers Media is converting to a public good and a nonprofit association in Q4.
share_topics: building a media company on-chain without learning to code
the three tools KFMEDIA uses to distribute creator media
using Unlock Protocol for subscriptions, memberships, and bookings
storing video forever on Arweave with Highlight.xyz
why a non-coder founder learned to vibe code

recaps.title: Owning your music as an independent artist
recaps.summary: An artist-track conversation with Joseph Goats (Jose Cabrera), a Venezuelan musician who has built a music life out of internet tools when there was no local industry to plug into - and, in 2017, not even a bank card. His model is what he calls digital street music: he plays for free, peer-to-peer, so anyone anywhere can support him directly. He traces the journey from discovering blockchain contests on Steemit (later Hive), to learning English along the way, to running impact concerts with Izzy and reimagining festivals for social impact and education. A wide-ranging talk on decentralization as a fractal - everyone has a fingerprint and something to say - on musicians as the bards who connect one community to the next, and on aligning music with nature and biology (Humberto Maturana and autopoiesis). He closes by playing a song.
takeaways: Digital street music: Jose plays for free, peer-to-peer, so anyone anywhere can support him directly - a model that worked when his country had no music infrastructure and, in 2017, no bank cards at all.

Decentralization as a fractal - everyone has a fingerprint and something to say, and the tools let each person create their own space and connect it to others. The real problem decentralization has to solve is fragmentation.
He entered through writing and blockchain contests (Steemit, later Hive), and watched the community fork Hive after a hostile takeover - proof that a community can branch and keep going.

Musicians as bards and nodes: the role of the artist is to travel between communities, vouch for people, and bring them together - turning a peer-to-peer connection into a real-world visit, tour, or festival.

He wants music aligned with nature and biology (Humberto Maturana, autopoiesis), so a song can carry knowledge to anyone regardless of titles - and the work that matters is reimagining festivals for social impact and education.
share_topics: digital street music and getting paid peer-to-peer
surviving as an artist with no local music industry
decentralization as a fractal where everyone has something to say
musicians as the bards connecting communities
aligning music with nature and biology

recaps.summary: A ZABAL Gamez workshop with Chris (chriscocreated), founder of Sopha - a Farcaster client built for depth and high signal - on what he built and why curation is needed. Sopha is an intentional third place, a coffee shop or library for the deeper writing, art, and thinking that scrolls past on the main feed, with a curated feed kept by the community's curators plus BradQ's The Pulse. Chris traces why he built it (when Farcaster felt like it was tipping into meme coins, he set out to protect the culture of interesting thinkers, and good vibe coding made him the one to do it), how easy it now is to build your own client on Farcaster's open data with Neynar and Hypersnap, and the nested stack of client, mini apps, and Snaps. He closes on where Sopha is headed: a second round of Purple funding, experiments in rewarding curators, infra, and live cultural-center experiences, with a thoughtfully designed, non-extractive token only when the time is right.
takeaways: Curation is the point - Sopha is a Farcaster client for depth, an intentional third place for high-signal writing and art, not a replacement for the daily feed.
Building your own client is now a few hours of work: Farcaster's open data plus Neynar (and Hypersnap) let anyone shape their own view.
You are not beholden to the algorithm - you build your own lens. That is the unlock for artists, writers, and creators.
The nested stack: a client is just a website showing Farcaster data; below it sit mini apps, and Snaps you can spin up in seconds.
The feed is curated by community curators (earn the role by showing taste), and BradQ runs a dedicated Pulse feed.
Sopha's next year (a second Purple round): reward curators, cover infra, run live cultural-center experiences, and design any token carefully and non-extractively.
Find it: @chriscocreated and @sopha on Farcaster, sopha.social.

punching above your weight by having AI read the papers first
sub-agents and adversarial agents as skills
one Bonfire, many agents: a federated crew sharing context
recaps.summary: A live ZABAL Gamez fireside between Zaal and Carlos (Plat0x, the Bonfires developer). Half working-session - the Bonfires rewrite (deadline June 14), ZAO's second Bonfire agent, auto-pushing context to the Bonfire on every git commit, and the positioning of Bonfires as a 'sense-making tool' where the agent is the librarian of a library - and half vibe-coding masterclass for the builder track: plan-then-goal with a measurable threshold, Bob-and-Alice diagramming before code, types-first prompting with a protocol/adapter contract, documentation-as-code, the Linus Torvalds compiler analogy, and Carlos's three skills (plan-making, sub-agents, adversarial agents).
takeaways: Plan-then-goal: give Claude a plan plus one goal with a measurable threshold (e.g. under 5s query latency) so it runs until it hits the number.
Diagram Bob and Alice before any code - define the exchange and the sequence first, language last.
Prompt types-first ('define types in Python for an app that does X') and use a protocol/adapter contract so you can swap the database later without a rewrite.
Documentation-as-code: remove all the code and the docs alone should reproduce it 1:1.
Carlos's three skills: plan-making (built from papers), sub-agents (the senior-dev orchestrator), and adversarial agents (one for, one against).

recaps.title: Starting and growing your own livestream
recaps.summary: Day 2 of ZABAL Gamez and the first creator-track session. Ohnahji (Brian), co-founder of the Ohnahji community - a 2022 project that grew into "blockchain's first HBCU," a safe space for Black, brown, and allied folks learning emerging tech - joins Zaal on Twitch to talk about starting and growing a livestream. Ohnahji is about 288 days into a 365 daily-stream journey. The throughline: consistency is king. Every tool glitches, the early days are frustrating for everyone, and you only get better by showing up. They cover Twitch's Streamed Together and shared chat for collaborating live, raids as a discovery and gamification tool, building your own chat bots, the builder basics of API keys and environment variables, and streaming opsec - be careful what you screen-share, and when in doubt, slow down.
takeaways: Consistency is king. Every tool glitches and the first weeks are frustrating for everyone - you only improve by streaming daily and not quitting.
Embrace the tech that already works. Web2 distribution (Twitch) still reaches people; bring things on-chain only when on-chain is genuinely better.
Twitch Streamed Together / Knock plus shared chat lets you collaborate live and merge chats; raids are a powerful community-discovery and gamification tool.
Build your own bots and tooling - API keys are account-scoped secrets; keep them in .env files, never published and never on screen.
Operational security: double-check what you screen-share, and when in doubt slow down - nine times out of ten rushing is the mistake.
share_topics: starting and growing your own livestream
why consistency is king
the tools - OBS, Restream, StreamYard - and that they all glitch
Twitch Streamed Together and merging chats live
raids as a community-discovery tool
building your own chat bots

recaps.title: Bonfire and the ZABAL Bonfire bot
share_topics: Bonfire as the ecosystem's shared memory
a knowledge graph that grows just by using your agents
the data-loss problem at conferences and communities
curation as the last scarce asset
hackathons that produce one interoperable protocol
querying the ZABAL Bonfire bot live
everyone gets an agent, with Bonfire as the orchestrator
the curators own the knowledge they build
recaps.summary: The second ZABAL Gamez workshop. Joshua.eth and Plat0x (Carlos) introduce Bonfire - a shared AI workspace where a group shares agents and, in the process of using them, grows a shared memory: a knowledge graph that becomes a collective, ownable asset. They get into the data-loss problem, why curation is the last scarce asset, a vision for hackathons where every project is interoperable by design, and a live look at querying the ZABAL Bonfire bot.
takeaways: Bonfire is the ecosystem's shared memory - the layer bots and builds sit on top of.
A group grows a knowledge graph just by using its agents, and the curators own it.
Curation is the last scarce asset: structured taste and knowledge stay unique and ownable.
The ZABAL Bonfire bot is live and queryable, so anyone joining mid-season can get caught up.

your main empire is the tree, sub-empires are the branches
the token is the amplifier, not the foundation
recaps.summary: Part 2 of the first ZABAL Gamez workshop - the live build that follows the Part 1 framework talk. yerbearserker (Jordan Oram) of Empire Builder gives a hands-on tour of the Empire tool stack from inside Farcaster, walks the top empires and how leaderboards score holders times boosters, runs a live Warplet raffle airdrop to DotA NFT holders, adds a $Zabal token booster, and stands up a fresh tokenless ZABAL Gamez empire on the spot, then wires two leaderboards (channel activity in /zabal and cast reactions).
takeaways: See a tokenless empire stood up live, start to finish.
Leaderboards score a base component multiplied by boosters - NFT holdings, token thresholds, or staking.
Distribution in practice: a live Warplet raffle airdrop to the most active DotA NFT holders.
A $Zabal token booster added live (10M minimum, 3x), funded from the treasury.

His message: build the foundation before you launch anything.
takeaways: Do not launch a token until you are ready - build the foundation first.
The Triple-A framework: Assemble, Affirm, Ascend.
Leaderboards, boosters, staking, and tactical generosity power a tokenless Empire.
yerbearserker stood up a tokenless ZABAL Gamez Empire live during the session.

share_topics: the ZAO Fractal, the first ZAO project
soulbound ZAO Respect earned weekly through peer consensus
the four objectives: empowerment, transparency, sustainability, equitable distribution
the Respect game: four-minute updates, then on-chain consensus
five ways to contribute, from building to onboarding
camera on earns ten extra ZAO Respect
recaps.summary: Zaal's weekly introduction to the ZAO Fractal, framed as the first ZAO project you can build on. He covers what the ZAO is - a decentralized impact network for the creator economy, the Z Talent Artist Organization - and how it runs on soulbound, illiquid ZAO Respect earned weekly through peer consensus. He walks the four objectives (empowerment, transparency, sustainability, equitable distribution) and the Respect game itself: breakout groups of up to six, four-minute updates, then on-chain consensus. The ZAO Fractal meets weekly at 6pm Eastern at discord.theZAO.com.
takeaways: The ZAO is a decentralized impact network for the creator economy, governed by soulbound, illiquid ZAO Respect earned weekly through peer consensus - not bought.
Four objectives anchor it: empowerment, transparency, sustainability, equitable distribution.
The Respect game: groups of up to six share four-minute updates, then rank by consensus (level six down to one), submitted on chain.
Five ways to contribute: contributions, collaborations, innovations, onboarding, and supporting community members.
Meets weekly, 6pm Eastern at discord.theZAO.com - turning your camera on earns an extra ten ZAO Respect.
